IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): TextFlow tackles an important and practical problem—measuring text similarity with explicit sequential structure—and evaluates across multiple standard datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval) showing encouraging results, which suggests utility for paraphrase, entailment, and relevance tasks. However, based solely on the summary, the contribution appears as a solid methodological alternative rather than a paradigm shift: the idea extends existing sequence-alignment intuitions and relies on tunable parameters (α, β, γ) and an XF layer, but lacks reported open code, explicit limitations, or broad evidence of outperforming strong neural baselines. Consequently, the paper is likely to be useful and adopted in the subfield but not immediately transformative across NLP more broadly. | B(3): TextFlow tackles an important and longstanding problem—capturing sequential information beyond n-gram overlap—by proposing a novel continuous-curve representation and a learnable XF layer, and it demonstrates promise across multiple standard datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval). The idea is conceptually interesting and could stimulate work on position-aware similarity measures and hybrid analytical/learned similarity layers, but without clear evidence of broad empirical superiority, ablation of assumptions, or released code its immediate influence on practice is uncertain. Thus the paper is likely to generate moderate follow-up interest and new research directions rather than immediately changing how the field approaches text similarity.

Consequently, the paper is likely to be useful and adopted in the subfield but not immediately transformative across NLP more broadly.

The idea is conceptually interesting and could stimulate work on position-aware similarity measures and hybrid analytical/learned similarity layers, but without clear evidence of broad empirical superiority, ablation of assumptions, or released code its immediate influence on practice is uncertain. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP problem (text similarity) and proposes a novel, interpretable similarity measure evaluated on standard benchmarks (SNLI, multiple RTE sets, SemEval) which aligns well with top-tier NLP/ML audiences. From an empirical-methodology perspective it largely meets venue expectations by comparing to a broad set of baselines and reporting hyperparameters, but the lack of mentioned code release and no stated limitations or extensive reproducibility details (e.g., seeds, train/dev/test splits, statistical tests) are minor concerns that temper a perfect fit. Overall the topic, datasets, and evaluation framing make it a good fit, pending stronger reproducibility commitments. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP problem—textual similarity—with a novel representational idea (continuous-curve TextFlow) and proposes both canonical and learnable parameterizations (XF layer), which is directly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (SNLI, multiple RTEs, SemEval datasets) and compares to a wide range of classical and sequence-aware baselines, using appropriate metrics, so the audience and evaluation standards are well matched. The framing (inspired by sequence alignment) and inclusion of both analytical and learned variants make it of clear interest to researchers in semantic similarity and representation learning.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP problem (text similarity) and proposes a novel, interpretable similarity measure evaluated on standard benchmarks (SNLI, multiple RTE sets, SemEval) which aligns well with top-tier NLP/ML audiences. From an empirical-methodology perspective it largely meets venue expectations by comparing to a broad set of baselines and reporting hyperparameters, but the lack of mentioned code release and no stated limitations or extensive reproducibility details (e.g., seeds, train/dev/test splits, statistical tests) are minor concerns that temper a perfect fit. Overall the topic, datasets, and evaluation framing make it a good fit, pending stronger reproducibility commitments.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem—textual similarity—with a novel representational idea (continuous-curve TextFlow) and proposes both canonical and learnable parameterizations (XF layer), which is directly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (SNLI, multiple RTEs, SemEval datasets) and compares to a wide range of classical and sequence-aware baselines, using appropriate metrics, so the audience and evaluation standards are well matched. The framing (inspired by sequence alignment) and inclusion of both analytical and learned variants make it of clear interest to researchers in semantic similarity and representation learning.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a distinct representation (continuous curves / DNA-inspired dot-matrix) and an XF scoring layer with learnable coefficients, which provides a moderately new perspective beyond classic n-gram and edit-distance approaches. However, the core ideas (sequence-alignment-inspired matching, position-aware similarity, and learning combination weights) are close extensions of well-studied methods rather than a radical departure. The contribution is a non-trivial engineering/design variant that an expert would find moderately informative but not highly surprising given existing alignment and learned-similarity literature. | B(3): The paper introduces a distinct framing—representing text pairs as continuous curves inspired by DNA dot-matrix alignment—and combines position-aware sequence matching with tunable coefficients (α,β,γ) and an XF layer to fit task data. However, the core operations draw directly on well-established alignment and sequence-similarity ideas (LCS, edit distances, dot-matrix), and the learned XF layer appears to be an elementary parameterization rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic mechanism. Thus the contribution is a noteworthy conceptual reframing with some engineering novelty, but it reads as a moderate, incremental advance rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a distinct representation (continuous curves / DNA-inspired dot-matrix) and an XF scoring layer with learnable coefficients, which provides a moderately new perspective beyond classic n-gram and edit-distance approaches. However, the core ideas (sequence-alignment-inspired matching, position-aware similarity, and learning combination weights) are close extensions of well-studied methods rather than a radical departure. The contribution is a non-trivial engineering/design variant that an expert would find moderately informative but not highly surprising given existing alignment and learned-similarity literature.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a distinct framing—representing text pairs as continuous curves inspired by DNA dot-matrix alignment—and combines position-aware sequence matching with tunable coefficients (α,β,γ) and an XF layer to fit task data. However, the core operations draw directly on well-established alignment and sequence-similarity ideas (LCS, edit distances, dot-matrix), and the learned XF layer appears to be an elementary parameterization rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic mechanism. Thus the contribution is a noteworthy conceptual reframing with some engineering novelty, but it reads as a moderate, incremental advance rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (TextFlow, α/β/γ parametrization, and the XF layer) with a clear methodological description tying continuous-curve representation to sequence matching. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and experiments across eight datasets (SNLI, RTEs, SemEval, etc.) and a new CORE metric are clearly presented. Presentation has minor gaps: no limitations are stated and code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables/examples are consistently effective, so a few clarifications or explicit artifacts (plots, examples, released code) would improve reproducibility and clarity. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, making the proposed TextFlow approach easy to locate. The writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and hyperparameters are reported, which supports reproducibility; however the summary lacks mention of figures/tables effectiveness and does not state limitations, and it is unclear whether all notation and examples are consistently defined throughout—minor clarifications would make the presentation exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (TextFlow, α/β/γ parametrization, and the XF layer) with a clear methodological description tying continuous-curve representation to sequence matching. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and experiments across eight datasets (SNLI, RTEs, SemEval, etc.) and a new CORE metric are clearly presented. Presentation has minor gaps: no limitations are stated and code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables/examples are consistently effective, so a few clarifications or explicit artifacts (plots, examples, released code) would improve reproducibility and clarity.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, making the proposed TextFlow approach easy to locate. The writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and hyperparameters are reported, which supports reproducibility; however the summary lacks mention of figures/tables effectiveness and does not state limitations, and it is unclear whether all notation and examples are consistently defined throughout—minor clarifications would make the presentation exceptional.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments cover multiple standard benchmarks (e.g., SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval) and compare against a broad set of baselines, which is good, but the summary indicates gaps: ablation/sensitivity analyses for canonical versus learned (α,β,γ) and for the XF layer are not clearly reported. Related work coverage is adequate, yet no failure cases or limitations are discussed and code/reproducibility details (algorithm pseudocode, seeds, dataset splits, release) are missing despite hyperparameter reporting. Overall the empirical evaluation is promising but lacks critical ablations, robustness analyses, and reproducibility artifacts needed to fully support the claims. | B(3): The paper evaluates TextFlow across multiple datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval tasks) and compares to a broad set of baselines, which provides reasonable empirical grounding for the main claim. However, the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses for the canonical versus learned (XF layer) parameterizations, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed—both important for a new similarity representation. Related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but absence of code and missing analyses keep the work from being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover multiple standard benchmarks (e.g., SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval) and compare against a broad set of baselines, which is good, but the summary indicates gaps: ablation/sensitivity analyses for canonical versus learned (α,β,γ) and for the XF layer are not clearly reported. Related work coverage is adequate, yet no failure cases or limitations are discussed and code/reproducibility details (algorithm pseudocode, seeds, dataset splits, release) are missing despite hyperparameter reporting. Overall the empirical evaluation is promising but lacks critical ablations, robustness analyses, and reproducibility artifacts needed to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates TextFlow across multiple datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval tasks) and compares to a broad set of baselines, which provides reasonable empirical grounding for the main claim. However, the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses for the canonical versus learned (XF layer) parameterizations, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed—both important for a new similarity representation. Related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but absence of code and missing analyses keep the work from being fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a plausible new similarity measure and reports experiments across several standard datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval) and multiple baselines, but the summary exposes gaps that weaken technical confidence. Key methodological details are missing or unclear: the claimed eight datasets vs. six listed, no mention of code release, limited description of the XF learning layer (identity activation) and how α/β/γ are optimized, and no evidence of statistical testing, multiple random seeds, or thorough ablation studies to isolate the contribution of sequential curve representation. Because of these reproducibility and experimental-control shortcomings (and the absence of reported significance or robustness analyses), the claims are partially supported but require additional empirical rigor to be fully validated. | B(3): The paper states clear main claims and reports experiments on six widely used datasets comparing against a broad set of baselines, which lends some empirical support. However, from the provided summary there are gaps: the continuous-curve formalism and the XF layer lack presented proofs or rigorous justification, and it is unclear whether statistical tests or ablations were run to validate gains and hyperparameter sensitivity. The DNA-alignment analogy and the claim of exploiting sequential information beyond n-grams appear plausible but rest on assumptions that are not shown to be theoretically grounded or thoroughly controlled for in the experiments.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible new similarity measure and reports experiments across several standard datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval) and multiple baselines, but the summary exposes gaps that weaken technical confidence. Key methodological details are missing or unclear: the claimed eight datasets vs. six listed, no mention of code release, limited description of the XF learning layer (identity activation) and how α/β/γ are optimized, and no evidence of statistical testing, multiple random seeds, or thorough ablation studies to isolate the contribution of sequential curve representation. Because of these reproducibility and experimental-control shortcomings (and the absence of reported significance or robustness analyses), the claims are partially supported but require additional empirical rigor to be fully validated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear main claims and reports experiments on six widely used datasets comparing against a broad set of baselines, which lends some empirical support. However, from the provided summary there are gaps: the continuous-curve formalism and the XF layer lack presented proofs or rigorous justification, and it is unclear whether statistical tests or ablations were run to validate gains and hyperparameter sensitivity. The DNA-alignment analogy and the claim of exploiting sequential information beyond n-grams appear plausible but rest on assumptions that are not shown to be theoretically grounded or thoroughly controlled for in the experiments.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper compares TextFlow against a broad set of classical string- and overlap-based baselines (Cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, etc.) across multiple benchmark datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval), which is appropriate for evaluating sequence-sensitive similarity. However, the summary indicates no comparison to recent neural/textual semantic baselines (e.g., SBERT/transformer-based sentence encoders or strong supervised paraphrase/entailment models), and it is unclear whether the novel CORE metric or training protocol yields strictly comparable evaluation conditions. The manuscript documents hyperparameters and related work coverage is adequate, but it does not state whether prior methods sometimes match or outperform TextFlow nor provide deep analysis of when gains arise—so the empirical comparison is useful but incomplete with respect to current state-of-the-art baselines. | B(4): The paper compares TextFlow to a broad set of classical and sequence-based baselines (cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, word-order + semantic combos) across multiple established datasets (SNLI, RTEs, SemEval), which is strong. However, the summary does not report comparisons to modern neural similarity models (e.g., transformer-based sentence encoders) and introduces a new evaluation metric (CORE) that could confound fairness; these are notable omissions. The authors acknowledge related work adequately and analyze differences in method, but lack of discussion on limitations and absence of certain recent baselines prevents a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares TextFlow against a broad set of classical string- and overlap-based baselines (Cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, etc.) across multiple benchmark datasets (SNLI, RTE variants, SemEval), which is appropriate for evaluating sequence-sensitive similarity. However, the summary indicates no comparison to recent neural/textual semantic baselines (e.g., SBERT/transformer-based sentence encoders or strong supervised paraphrase/entailment models), and it is unclear whether the novel CORE metric or training protocol yields strictly comparable evaluation conditions. The manuscript documents hyperparameters and related work coverage is adequate, but it does not state whether prior methods sometimes match or outperform TextFlow nor provide deep analysis of when gains arise—so the empirical comparison is useful but incomplete with respect to current state-of-the-art baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares TextFlow to a broad set of classical and sequence-based baselines (cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, word-order + semantic combos) across multiple established datasets (SNLI, RTEs, SemEval), which is strong. However, the summary does not report comparisons to modern neural similarity models (e.g., transformer-based sentence encoders) and introduces a new evaluation metric (CORE) that could confound fairness; these are notable omissions. The authors acknowledge related work adequately and analyze differences in method, but lack of discussion on limitations and absence of certain recent baselines prevents a top score.

main_claims: Proposes a novel text similarity measure called TextFlow that represents input text pairs as continuous curves and uses word positions and sequence matching to compute similarity.
TextFlow is inspired by DNA sequence alignment representations (dot matrix) and exploits sequential information beyond traditional n-gram/skip-gram overlaps.
The measure includes canonical parameters (α, β, γ) which can be left as defaults or trained via a neural network with a dedicated XF layer to fit task-specific data.
Experiments on eight datasets show encouraging results in paraphrase detection, textual entailment recognition, and ranking relevance.
_summary.methodology: TextFlow represents two texts as continuous curves (inspired by DNA alignment) and computes similarity by combining actual word positions and sequence matching; parameters α, β, γ can be set to canonical defaults or learned using a neural network with a dedicated (identity-activated) XF layer that computes the XF value from coefficient vectors.
baselines: word order similarity + semantic similarity combinations (as in prior work)

reviewer_a.name: Empirical Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP systems in both industry and academia, focusing on reproducible experimentation and rigorous empirical comparisons.
traits: checks for complete and fair baselines, strong ablation studies, and proper hyperparameter tuning protocols
demands reproducibility: clear algorithms, pseudocode, dataset splits, seed control, and code/data release
evaluates statistical validity: confidence intervals, significance testing, and robustness across datasets and random seeds
examines implementation details and computational costs (runtime, memory), and sensitivity to parameter choices (α, β, γ)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes methodological rigor and reproducibility, highlighting missing experiments or controls needed to support empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Senior NLP Researcher — Semantic Similarity
reviewer_b.background: Professor-level researcher who has led multiple projects on textual similarity, paraphrase detection, and semantic representation, and who follows advances in algorithms and theory for sequence matching.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work (n-grams, dynamic time warping, alignment/dot-matrix methods, neural similarity layers) and whether the paper meaningfully advances the field
values clear articulation of conceptual contribution, theoretical grounding (e.g., justification for continuous-curve representation), and limitations of the analogy to DNA alignment
looks for practical impact: how the approach integrates with current NLP pipelines, scalability to large corpora, and applicability across tasks beyond reported datasets
notices presentation clarity: whether motivations, assumptions, and relation to existing similarity measures are honestly and precisely stated
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on conceptual novelty, literature placement, and broader significance—clarifying whether the idea advances understanding or practice in text similarity.
